I didn’t watch it because it’s incredibly dumb Edit for the people from asking why the award show is dumb: Basically the NBA tries to make everything a spectacle. The last game of the regular season was on April 11th. That’s about the same time the voting for the awards took place. Instead of revealing it when they knew the results, they waited until after the playoffs and the draft and had the award show a few days ago. It shoehorned in celebrities and musical acts to push viewership outside of NBA fans to maximize advertising dollars. To make it worse the Coach of the year Award went to a guy who got fired after his team lost in the playoffs. He had to go up and make a speech. I get that it’s a business/financial move because advertisers will pay a ton of money but fans are mad they wait so long to reveal the results solely for the benefit of the NBA. TL;DR: Awards are good. Making them into a spectacle 2.5 months after voting is not.

text: No, not quite. There are two types of stats: descriptive stats and predictive stats. A descriptive stat can be completely legitimate and have no predictive value. Now, these two types of stats are not mutually exclusive. Line-up specific net rating stats are somewhere between descriptive & predictive. Whether we want to consider this stat to be more descriptive or predictive is up for debate. And without getting deep into the math, I fear such a debate would boil down to a lot of hot takes rather than substance. I will say that there were 4 teams who reached 40 games before 20 losses over their last 60 regular season games. The Pacers & OKC were 2 of the 4. The starting lineups of the other two teams to accomplish this fate - Boston and Cleveland - also have starting lineups with very high net ratings. (Using the last 60 games instead of the first 60 games because the Pacers were so hurt over their first 25 games that they weren't even really able to play these lineups. Also, supposedly, performance in the last 60 is more predictive of playoff potential than performance in the first 60 but heavy emphasis on the "supposedly".)
label: analysis

text: Thunder have averaged 21.4 wide open (meaning the closest defender was 6+ feet away) 3s per game over the course of the series according to NBA.com. Nuggets have averaged 20.2. OKC is averaging 12.8 open (closest defender is 4-6 feet away) 3s per game, whereas Denver is averaging 15.6. OKC is averaging 2.6 tight (closest defender is 2-4 feet away) whereas Denver is averaging 4.2. So OKC is averaging 1.2 more wide open 3s, 2.8 fewer open 3s, and 1.6 fewer tight 3s. Believe what you want, but actual stats show Denver is giving more space to shooters than OKC.

text: Pacers are at 80 points, and Cavs are on 39 points (41 deficit). There's 24 minutes of the game left, so that's 60 \* 24 second possessions left. If we give Pacers half and Cavs half, that means 30 possessions left each. The NBA average is about 112 points per 100 possessions, which would be 33.6 points for 30 possessions. So \*\*IF\*\* both teams used the full 24 seconds of each possession, and if Pacers scored zero while Cavs scored 33.6 points - Cavs would still lose by 7.4 points. Obviously this doesn't factor in a lot of things, quick possessions, turnovers, foul game, Donovan Mitchell going off, etc.
label: analysis
